topic: Exponential Growth in Computing Power: A Comparative Analysis of Apollo 11's Guidance Computer and Modern Smartphones
title: A Comparative Study of Computing Power in Apollo 11 and Modern Smartphones

lesson.learn: Modern smartphones possess computing power that exceeds the Apollo 11 Guidance Computer (AGC) by a factor of approximately 100 million. This significant disparity can be attributed to the exponential growth in computing power, driven by advancements in computer hardware and software development.
lesson.simpler: The computing power of modern smartphones is equivalent to that of multiple AGCs, the primary computer used in the Apollo 11 mission. This is made possible by improvements in transistor density, advancements in programming languages, and the development of efficient algorithms.
lesson.keyTakeaway: The most significant insight to remember is that the computing power of modern smartphones is a direct result of the exponential growth in transistor density, which has led to a corresponding increase in computing performance and memory capacity.
lesson.deeperDive: One key aspect contributing to this growth in computing power is Moore's Law, which states that the number of transistors on a microchip doubles approximately every two years. This has led to an exponential increase in computing performance and memory capacity. For instance, the AGC used on Apollo 11 had a clock speed of approximately 1 MHz and 2,048 bytes of memory. Modern smartphones, on the other hand, have a clock speed of billions of hertz and gigabytes of RAM. Furthermore, advancements in programming languages, algorithms, and software engineering have also played a significant role in making modern smartphones incredibly powerful. The use of parallel processing, which enables faster computation by executing multiple instructions simultaneously, has become a standard feature in modern smartphones.
lesson.funFact: The first smartphone, the IBM Simon Personal Communicator, was released in 1994 and featured a touchscreen display, email capabilities, and a fax machine. However, this device lacked many of the features and capabilities that are taken for granted in modern smartphones.

people.name: Gordon Moore

people.name: Martin Cooper

concepts.name: Moore's Law
concepts.description: The observation that the number of transistors on a microchip doubles approximately every two years, leading to exponential growth in computing performance and memory capacity.

concepts.name: Transistor density increase
concepts.description: The increase in the number of transistors on a microchip, which has led to a corresponding increase in computing performance and memory capacity.

concepts.name: Parallel processing
concepts.description: A technique used to improve computation speed by executing multiple instructions simultaneously.

concepts.name: Algorithmic advancements
concepts.description: Improvements in algorithms that have led to more efficient computation and better use of computer resources.
